function.name: web_search
function.description: Search the public internet for information. Each result will contain a url, a title, and one excerpt taken directly from the page.

start_date.description: (optional) The start date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. Must not be equal to end_date.

end_date.description: (optional) The end date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. If the value is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

number_of_results.description: (optional) The number of search results to return.

function.name: retrieve_information
function.description: Retrieves critical data segments from a previously ingested document via hybrid lexical and semantic calculations. Crucial Requirement: The search prompt argument MUST contain the token placeholder containing your storage key inside double curly brackets, exactly format formatted as: {{key_name}} alongside your query description text.

prompt.description: Your target question containing the explicit target reference mapping (e.g., 'What were the total research costs in {{my_document_key}}?').

preserve_document_order.description: Set to True if processing complex structural data dependencies across sequential disclosures.

function.name: parse_html_page
function.description: Parses an HTML document or SEC filing. It automatically extracts key metadata, converts asset tables to clean markdown, slices sections into optimal word chunks, and runs concurrent contextual LLM enrichment before updating the disk cache.

url.description: The target URL of the HTML page/filing to process.

key.description: The unique cache storage key for saving and loading the index.

function.name: edgar_search
function.description: Search the EDGAR Database through the SEC API. You should provide a search query. You can also optionally provide a start date, an end date, a page number, top N results, a list of form types, and/or a list of CIKs. The results are returned as a list of dictionaries, each containing the metadata for a filing. It does not contain the full text of the filing.

search_query.description: The case-insensitive search-term or phrase to search the contents of filings and their attachments. This can be a single word, phrase, or combination of words and phrases. Supported search features include wildcards (*), Boolean operators (OR, NOT), and exact phrase matching by enclosing phrases in quotation marks ("exact phrase"). By default, all terms are joined by an implicit AND operator.

form_types.description: (optional) Limits search to specific EDGAR form types (e.g., ['8-K', '10-Q']) list of strings. Default: all form types

ciks.description: (optional) Filters results to filings from specified CIKs, type list of strings. Leading zeros are optional but may be included. Example: [ "0001811414", "1318605" ]. Default: all CIKs

start_date.description: (optional) Start date for the search range in yyyy-mm-dd format. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

end_date.description: (optional) End date for the search range, in the same format as startDate. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

page.description: (optional) Used for pagination. Each page contains up to 100 matching filings. Increase the page number to retrieve the next set of 100 filings. Example: 3 retrieves the third page. Default: 1

top_n_results.description: (optional) Return only the first N results out of 100 from the page. If not provided, all 100 results will be returned. E.g. if page is 2, and top_n_results is 10, you will receive results 100 to 110.

function.name: calculator
function.description: Evaluate a mathematical expression and return the result. Use this tool for all arithmetic calculations instead of computing by hand. Supports: +, -, *, /, ** (exponentiation), % (modulo), and parentheses for grouping. Available functions: abs(), min(), max(), sqrt(), log(), log10(). Examples: '(5000 - 3200) * 0.21', '(2865507 / 1905871) ** 0.5 - 1', '14060 / 2148'.

function.name: price_history
function.description: Fetch historical daily price data for a specific asset class. Returns a CSV table with one row per day. Use asset_class='equity' or 'etf' for US-listed stocks/ETFs (e.g. AAPL, SPY), 'crypto' for pairs like btcusd (lowercase, no dash), or 'fx' for pairs like audusd. Non-US equities and most indices/futures are not covered by the pricing provider. Each row includes raw OHLC (open/high/low/close), split- and dividend-adjusted OHLC (adjOpen/adjHigh/adjLow/adjClose), raw and adjusted volume, divCash (dividend amount on the date), and splitFactor.

ticker.description: Ticker symbol. High-level symbology rules: (1) Equity/ETF/mutual fund: use the US exchange-listed symbol (AAPL, SPY, VFIAX). Symbols are case-insensitive and contain only letters and dashes. (2) Share classes use a dash, not a dot: 'BRK-A' / 'BRK-B' (not 'BRK.A'), 'BF-B'. (3) Securities are identified by the ticker as listed on the primary US exchange; non-US listings, OTC pink sheets, indices, and futures are generally not covered. (4) Tickers can be reused after delisting; data is point-in-time for the entity that held the ticker on each date. (5) Crypto: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'btcusd', 'ethusd'. (6) FX: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'audusd', 'eurusd'.

asset_class.description: Asset class used to route to the proper pricing endpoint.

function.name: submit_final_result
function.description: Submits the final answer to the user. You should include your final answer, as well as any necessary reasoning, justification, calculations, and explanation. Finally, you should provide any sources used to answer the question. You MUST use this tool to submit your final result. The user will not see your response if you do not use this tool to submit. You will not be able to continue working after this tool is called; the conversation will be ended.

final_result.description: The final result to submit to the user

steps.message:
You are an IPO and financial diligence agent.

You are given a question and must answer it using the tools provided.

You cannot interact with the user or ask clarifying questions.

Answer solely based on available information and tools.

Assume the current date is May 31, 2026.

---

TOOLS AND DATA SOURCES

You have access to a document storage system.

You may store parsed HTML retrieved from the web and later retrieve
information using LLM-based retrieval prompts.

Use this system to avoid context-window limitations.

When working with long filings, prefer section-specific retrieval prompts
rather than broad document-level retrieval.

Examples:

- retrieve the Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation table from MD&A
- retrieve the glossary definition of Starshield
- retrieve the controlled-company governance disclosure

For peer-comparison questions:

- retrieve peer SEC filings using edgar_search
- do not rely on memory for peer financial figures

For historical public-equity price data not available in SEC filings:

- use price_history as primary source
- use raw unadjusted prices unless adjusted prices are requested
- fall back to web_search only if price_history fails

Stock indices are not covered by price_history.

Use authoritative sources such as FRED or web_search for index levels.

---

SOURCE HIERARCHY

Prioritize sources in this order:

1. S-1 / S-1A filings
2. Incorporated SEC filings
3. SEC exhibits and merger documents
4. SEC periodic filings
5. Company earnings materials
6. External sources

If figures differ across sources:

prefer the most authoritative SEC source containing the directly reported
figure and explain material discrepancies when relevant.

SEC filings are the primary and most authoritative source of financial and
disclosure information.

---

ANSWER STANDARDS

You must call submit_final_result with the completed answer.

Your submission will not be processed unless submit_final_result is called.

Include:

- complete reasoning
- calculations
- supporting analysis

You will be evaluated on:

- final-answer accuracy
- analytical correctness

Do not round intermediate calculations.

Round only final reported values.

When possible, report calculated figures to at least two decimal places.

Report financial figures using the same scale and units used in the filing
unless otherwise specified.

Share prices should be reported with two decimal places.

---

DISCLOSURE DISCIPLINE

Before reaching a conclusion:

determine whether the filing provides sufficient information to answer:

- directly
- partially
- only through inference

Distinguish carefully among:

- facts explicitly disclosed
- reasonable filing-supported inferences
- information not disclosed

Do not speculate beyond what the filing supports.

If material information is absent:

explicitly state that the filing does not provide sufficient information to
conclude.

Absence of disclosure may itself be analytically material.

---

FORWARD-LOOKING STATEMENTS

Treat separately:

- historical facts
- roadmap targets
- commercialization expectations
- management intentions
- forward-looking statements

Do not present aspirations or plans as historical outcomes.

---

GOVERNANCE AND LEGAL QUESTIONS

For governance, legal, or disclosure-language questions:

use the filing's precise terminology whenever possible.

Distinguish among:

- requirements
- exemptions
- company elections
- forward-looking language

---

ACCOUNTING POLICY QUESTIONS

For accounting-policy questions:

1. Identify the relevant disclosed accounting policy or treatment
2. Determine whether the filing explicitly resolves the issue
3. If unresolved, state that the filing does not specify treatment

---

NON-GAAP QUESTIONS

For non-GAAP measures:

identify:

- the starting GAAP metric
- each disclosed adjustment
- whether economically recurring costs are excluded

Do not accept non-GAAP measures without analyzing their construction.

---

AUDIENCE TAGS

Questions may include audience tags such as:

[PUBLIC | BANK]
[VC | CREDIT]
[LEGAL | ACCOUNTING]

Use these tags to calibrate terminology and analytical emphasis while
remaining grounded in disclosed facts.

---

SOURCES

At the end of your answer provide:

{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "...",
      "name": "..."
    }
  ]
}


---


FILING CONTEXT

The primary filing for this benchmark is the SpaceX S-1 filed May 20, 2026:

https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm

Important:

The consolidated financial statements have been retrospectively recast under
common-control accounting to include:

- xAI (merged February 2, 2026)
- X Holdings (merged into xAI March 28, 2025)

for all historical periods presented.

No period reflects standalone SpaceX operations exclusively.

When interpreting historical segment figures, state whether they reflect the
recast consolidated entity.

SpaceX (SPCX) has no public trading history as of May 30, 2026.

Do not attempt price_history retrieval for SpaceX or SPCX.

For peer valuation or peer financial benchmarking, retrieve peer-company SEC
filings using edgar_search rather than relying on memory.

Question:
The xAI merger became effective February 2, 2026 and the X merger March 28, 2025. For how many months of FY2025 was each entity included on an actual-close basis, and what analytical challenge does retrospective recast accounting create?

steps.reasoning_content:
Let me analyze this question carefully. The user is asking about:

1. For how many months of FY2025 was each entity (xAI and X) included on an actual-close basis
2. What analytical challenge does retrospective recast accounting create

Let me first retrieve information from the SpaceX S-1 filing to confirm the merger dates and understand the recast accounting treatment.

From the question itself and the filing context:
- xAI merger effective February 2, 2026
- X Holdings (merged into xAI) effective March 28, 2025

For FY2025 (which would be January 1, 2025 to December 31, 2025):


Since xAI merged on February 2, 2026, it has zero months of inclusion in FY2025 on an actual-close basis. X Holdings, which merged on March 28, 2025, would have approximately 9 months of inclusion through year-end. Now I need to pull the S-1 filing to verify these dates and understand how the retrospective recast accounting affects the comparability of financial data across periods.

results.content: SUCCESS: Loaded portable assets for key: spacex_s1_main

steps.reasoning_content: Good, the document is loaded. Now let me retrieve information about the merger dates and the recast accounting treatment.

arguments.prompt: In {{spacex_s1_main}}, retrieve the merger dates for xAI and X Holdings, including when each merger became effective and when X was merged into xAI. Also retrieve the explanation of retrospective recast accounting and how the financial statements have been restated to include these entities for historical periods.

results.content:
Based on the provided text, here are the key details regarding the xAI and X mergers and their accounting treatment:

### Merger Dates
*   **xAI Merger (SpaceX acquires xAI):** Effective **February 2, 2026**.
*   **X Merger (xAI acquires X and X.AI Corp.):** Effective **March 28, 2025**.
    *   X.AI Corp. began operations in March 2023, and Twitter (later X) was acquired by Elon Musk in October 2022.

### Accounting Treatment: Common-Control Reorganization & Retrospective Combination
The mergers were accounted for as **reorganizations of entities under common control** because Elon Musk held a controlling financial interest (majority voting interest) in SpaceX, xAI, and X throughout the periods presented.

As a result, the following retrospective accounting principles were applied:
1.  **Combined Historical Financials:** SpaceX's consolidated financial statements were prepared to reflect the **retrospective combination** of the net assets of SpaceX, xAI, and X for all historical periods presented.
2.  **No New Goodwill or Intangibles:** No new goodwill or other intangible assets were recorded as part of the mergers.
3.  **Elimination of Intercompany Transactions:** All historical related party transactions between the entities were eliminated in consolidation.
4.  **Combined Equity:** The capital stock and shareholders’ equity for all periods presented reflect the continuation of the historical SpaceX capital stock and shareholders’ equity, combined with the historical capital stock and shareholders’ equity of X and xAI, adjusted for the merger exchange ratios.
5.  **Change in Reporting Entity:** This treatment constitutes a **change in reporting entity**, meaning the financial statements for prior periods are presented as if the combined company (SpaceX, xAI, and X) had always existed.

**Important Exception:** While common stock was retrospectively combined, **xAI's Redeemable Convertible Preferred Stock** was *not* retrospectively recast for periods prior to the xAI Merger. It remained presented as a separate equity class of xAI until its conversion into SpaceX Common Stock occurred at the merger closing.

### Key References in the Text
The explanation is consistently repeated across multiple sections (Notes to the Financial Statements, Preamble, Summary Data). For the definitive accounting policy, the text refers to **Note 1 (Nature of Business)** and the related **Note 12 or 13 (Redeemable Convertible Preferred Stock and Shareholders’ Equity)**.

steps.reasoning_content: Now I have the key information. Let me also look for specific disclosure about the FY2025 period and any discussion of analytical challenges with the recast accounting.

arguments.prompt: In {{spacex_s1_main}}, retrieve any discussion of what periods each entity was actually part of SpaceX operations on a close-date basis versus recast basis. Look for disclosure about how many months xAI and X were included in the actual results versus retrospective restatement. Also look for any risk factors or discussion about the analytical challenges or limitations of the retrospective recast accounting for investors.

results.content:
# Analysis of Entity Inclusion Periods and Retrospective Recast Accounting

Based on a thorough review of all the provided prospectus fragments, here is what can be determined and what appears absent:

---

## What the Fragments Reveal About Actual vs. Recast Inclusion

### Close-Date Timeline

| Entity | Close Date | Acquired By |
|--------|-----------|-------------|
| Twitter | October 2022 | Elon Musk (personally) |
| X.AI Corp | March 2023 | Began operations |
| X Holdings Corp. (incl. X.AI Corp.) | March 28, 2025 | xAI ("X Merger") |
| xAI (incl. X) | February 2, 2026 | SpaceX ("xAI Merger") |

### Critical Structural Point

The fragments reveal an **indirect acquisition chain**: SpaceX never directly acquired X. Instead:

1. xAI acquired X on **March 28, 2025**
2. SpaceX acquired xAI on **February 2, 2026**

This means **X entered SpaceX's consolidated group only on February 2, 2026** — the date xAI became a wholly-owned subsidiary. Despite the X Merger closing nearly eleven months earlier, X's results would have flowed through xAI's standalone financial statements, not SpaceX's, until the xAI Merger closed.

### Actual Consolidated Periods

Based on the financial statement dates referenced throughout the fragments:

- **Audited consolidated balance sheet**: as of **December 31, 2025 and 2024** (from the index of financial statements)
- **Interim consolidated balance sheet**: as of **March 31, 2026**
- **Audited statements of operations**: years ended **December 31, 2025, 2024, and 2023**
- **Interim statements**: three months ended **March 31, 2026 and 2025**

This creates a meaningful distinction:

| Entity | First Day of Actual SpaceX Consolidation | Approximate Months of Actual Results (through 3/31/2026) |
|--------|------------------------------------------|----------------------------------------------------------|
| xAI | February 2, 2026 | ~2 months |
| X (via xAI) | February 2, 2026 | ~2 months |

Yet all financial statements — including years ended December 31, 2023, 2024, and 2025 — are presented as if SpaceX had always consolidated xAI and X. This means:

- **For the year ended December 31, 2023**: ~12 months of purely retrospective recast data; zero actual months of consolidation
- **For the year ended December 31, 2024**: ~12 months of purely retrospective recast data; zero actual months of consolidation
- **For the year ended December 31, 2025**: ~12 months of purely retrospective recast data; zero actual months of consolidation (the xAI Merger closed after year-end)
- **For the three months ended March 31, 2026**: ~2 months actual consolidation (February 2 – March 31, 2026); ~1 month recast (January 1 – February 1, 2026)

**In other words, by the time the prospectus was issued, approximately two months of actual combined operations existed across all historical periods presented.**

---

## What the Fragments Do NOT Contain

Despite careful review, the following are **not present** in the provided excerpts:

### 1. No Explicit Disclosure of Actual vs. Recast Month Counts

The fragments do not include a passage that says something like "xAI was included in our results for X months on an actual basis and Y months on a recast basis." The accounting treatment is described in technical terms (common control, retrospective combination, historical carrying amounts), but the practical informational implication — that investors are looking at financials that only recently became "real" in a consolidated sense — is left unstated.

### 2. No Risk Factor Addressing Analytical Limitations of the Recast

The one risk factor excerpt provided discusses:
- The early-stage nature of the AI segment
- Integration, scaling, and execution risks
- The AI segment being "recently formed" and "still being fully integrated and optimized"

However, this is a **business integration risk factor**, not a **financial reporting or analytical risk factor**. The fragments do not contain a risk factor warning investors that:

- The historical financial data is substantially a retrospective accounting construct rather than a record of actual consolidated operations
- Comparability across periods may be impaired because the recast combines entities that operated independently with different cost structures, capital allocation strategies, and related-party dynamics
- Historical revenue, expense, and profitability figures do not reflect what SpaceX shareholders actually experienced in those periods
- The recast eliminates intercompany transactions between entities that were under common control but operated as separate companies, potentially obscuring the true economic relationships

### 3. No Discussion of Intercompany Elimination Effects

The fragments state multiple times that "all historical related party transactions between the entities have been eliminated in consolidation," but there is no quantitative discussion of how significant these eliminations were, or what the financial statements would have looked like without them. For example, if xAI was paying X Holdings for data access, or if SpaceX was providing launch services to xAI at preferential rates, those transactions would have been eliminated — but the investor is given no sense of magnitude.

### 4. No Sensitivity or Pro Forma Comparison

There is no presentation of what the financials would look like on a standalone basis (i.e., SpaceX without xAI and X) versus the combined recast basis, which would be the most analytically useful comparison for an investor trying to understand the relative contributions of each business.

---

## The Accounting Rationale Provided

The fragments repeatedly explain *why* retrospective recast treatment is required:

> "The Mergers have been accounted for as reorganizations of entities under common control as Mr. Elon Musk had a controlling financial interest in the Company, xAI and X through his majority voting interest in each such entity during the periods presented."

This is technically correct under U.S. GAAP (ASC 805-50, Transactions Between Entities Under Common Control). When entities under common control are combined, the financial statements are recast as if the entities had always been combined, using historical carrying amounts. No goodwill is recognized, and intercompany transactions are eliminated.

However, the fragments also reveal an **exception** to full retrospective recasting:

- **xAI's Redeemable Convertible Preferred Stock** was NOT retrospectively recast into SpaceX Common Stock for periods prior to the xAI Merger. It was presented as outstanding preferred stock in historical periods and only converted as of February 2, 2026.
- This is described as being because "the xAI Redeemable Convertible Preferred Stock was legally outstanding during all historical periods prior to the xAI Merger and represented a separate equity class of a legally distinct predecessor entity."

This creates an inconsistency in the presentation: common stock is recast (as if always converted), but preferred stock is not recast (presented in its historical legal form). While this is defensible legally and under GAAP, it means that the historical equity structure shown in the financial statements is a hybrid — partly recast, partly historical — which could confuse investors analyzing dilution, share counts, or earnings per share across periods.

---

## Summary of Findings

| Question | Finding |
|----------|---------|
| Was a monthly breakdown of actual vs. recast inclusion provided? | **No** — not in the fragments |
| Were risk factors about recast accounting limitations included? | **No** — the risk factor cited addresses business integration, not reporting limitations |
| Did the fragments disclose standalone vs. combined financials? | **No** |
| Did the fragments quantify intercompany eliminations? | **No** |
| Was there any discussion of analytical comparability challenges? | **No** |

The fragments collectively present the retrospective recast as a technical accounting requirement without translating it into an investor-oriented disclosure about what the numbers actually represent in terms of the company's real operational history as a combined entity. An investor reading these sections would understand *that* the financials were recast and *why* (common control), but would lack the context to assess *how much* of the historical data reflects actual consolidated operations versus accounting convention.

steps.message: Now I have all the information needed to construct a thorough answer. Let me compile the analysis.
steps.reasoning_content:
This is very comprehensive. Now let me compile my answer.

Key facts:
1. xAI merger became effective February 2, 2026
2. X merger (X Holdings into xAI) became effective March 28, 2025
3. xAI was merged into SpaceX on February 2, 2026

For FY2025 (January 1, 2025 to December 31, 2025):
- xAI merger: February 2, 2026 — this is AFTER FY2025 ended. So xAI was not actually consolidated with SpaceX for any month of FY2025. It was included on a 12-month purely retrospective recast basis.


- X was acquired by xAI in March 2025, but since xAI itself wasn't part of SpaceX until February 2026, X was only part of SpaceX's consolidated results starting that date. So for FY2025, X was included retrospectively for all 12 months despite only being in the xAI group for about 9 months of that year.

The real issue here is that neither entity was actually part of SpaceX during FY2025 on an actual-close basis—both were only included through retrospective recasting. This creates a misleading picture for investors because they're looking at financials that don't reflect real consolidated operations, the elimination of intercompany transactions obscures what the economic relationships actually were, and there's no standalone comparison to show how SpaceX performed without these entities. Plus, the equity treatment is inconsistent, which makes historical comparisons even more confusing.

arguments.final_result:
## Actual-Close Inclusion Periods in FY2025

### xAI (merged into SpaceX effective February 2, 2026)
**Zero months of FY2025 on an actual-close basis.** The xAI Merger closed on February 2, 2026 — more than one month *after* FY2025 ended on December 31, 2025. Therefore, xAI was not part of SpaceX's consolidated group for any day of FY2025.

### X Holdings (merged into xAI effective March 28, 2025)
**Zero months of FY2025 on an actual-close basis from SpaceX's perspective.** Although X was acquired by xAI on March 28, 2025, xAI was itself a separate entity and not a SpaceX subsidiary until February 2, 2026. Since SpaceX is the reporting entity, X entered SpaceX's consolidated group only when xAI did — on February 2, 2026. X's results flowed through xAI's standalone financials for ~9 months of FY2025 (March 28 – December 31, 2025), but those xAI financials were then only combined into SpaceX's on a retrospective recast basis.

**Summary Table:**

| Entity | Close Date | Months in FY2025 (Actual-Consolidated with SpaceX) | Months in FY2025 (Retrospective Recast Only) |
|--------|-----------|-----------------------------------------------------|----------------------------------------------|
| xAI | Feb 2, 2026 | 0 | 12 |
| X Holdings (via xAI) | Feb 2, 2026 (indirect) | 0 | 12 |

All 12 months of FY2025 results for both entities appear in SpaceX's audited financial statements solely through retrospective recast accounting — not through actual consolidation.

---

## Analytical Challenge Created by Retrospective Recast Accounting

The retrospective recast, mandated because all entities were under common control of Elon Musk (ASC 805-50), creates several significant analytical challenges:

### 1. Historical Financials Are Not an Operational Record
The FY2025 (and FY2024 and FY2023) financial statements present combined figures for entities that were, in reality, independently operated separate companies during those periods. An investor analyzing "SpaceX FY2025 revenue" or "SpaceX FY2025 operating loss" is looking at a retrospective accounting construct, not a record of what SpaceX shareholders actually experienced. Only from February 2, 2026 onward do the consolidated results reflect actual combined operations.

### 2. Intercompany Eliminations Obscure True Economics
The filing states that "all historical related party transactions between the entities have been eliminated in consolidation," but no quantitative disclosure of the magnitude of these eliminations is provided. If, for example, SpaceX provided launch services to xAI at preferential rates, or xAI paid X for data access, those flows vanish from the recast financials — yet they were real economic relationships between entities operating independently. The investor has no way to assess what the standalone financials looked like versus the combined view.

### 3. No Standalone vs. Combined Comparison Available
The filing does not present what SpaceX's financials would look like on a standalone basis (excluding xAI and X) alongside the recast combined figures. Without this decomposition, an investor cannot determine the relative contribution of the legacy launch/satellite business versus the newer AI and social-media segments to historical revenue, costs, or profitability.

### 4. Hybrid Equity Treatment Creates Confusion
A particularly subtle challenge: while SpaceX common stock is recast as if it always existed in the combined form, xAI's Redeemable Convertible Preferred Stock was *not* retrospectively recast for periods prior to the xAI Merger. It is presented as outstanding preferred stock in historical periods and only converted as of February 2, 2026. This creates a hybrid equity presentation — partly recast, partly historical — that can distort analyses of historical dilution, share counts, and earnings per share.

### 5. Investor Comparability Is Impaired
Because the recast combines entities with different cost structures, capital allocation strategies, and risk profiles under a single historical set of financials, period-over-period comparability is weakened. Revenue growth rates, margin trends, and capital expenditure patterns in FY2025 reflect the combined recast entity, not the trajectory of SpaceX's core business that investors have tracked for years.

---

## Bottom Line

For FY2025, neither xAI nor X was included in SpaceX's consolidated financial statements on an actual-close basis — both entities' results appear entirely through retrospective recast. This means that 100% of the FY2025 financials presented in the S-1 for these entities are an accounting convention rather than a record of actual consolidated operations. The first month of actual combined results is February 2026, and even then, the Q1 2026 interim period includes ~1 month of recast (January 1 – February 1, 2026) alongside only ~2 months of actual consolidated operations.

**Sources:**
- SpaceX S-1 filing (May 20, 2026): https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm — Note 1 (Nature of Business), Summary Data, and Notes to Financial Statements regarding common-control accounting treatment

results.content:
## Actual-Close Inclusion Periods in FY2025

### xAI (merged into SpaceX effective February 2, 2026)
**Zero months of FY2025 on an actual-close basis.** The xAI Merger closed on February 2, 2026 — more than one month *after* FY2025 ended on December 31, 2025. Therefore, xAI was not part of SpaceX's consolidated group for any day of FY2025.

### X Holdings (merged into xAI effective March 28, 2025)
**Zero months of FY2025 on an actual-close basis from SpaceX's perspective.** Although X was acquired by xAI on March 28, 2025, xAI was itself a separate entity and not a SpaceX subsidiary until February 2, 2026. Since SpaceX is the reporting entity, X entered SpaceX's consolidated group only when xAI did — on February 2, 2026. X's results flowed through xAI's standalone financials for ~9 months of FY2025 (March 28 – December 31, 2025), but those xAI financials were then only combined into SpaceX's on a retrospective recast basis.

**Summary Table:**

| Entity | Close Date | Months in FY2025 (Actual-Consolidated with SpaceX) | Months in FY2025 (Retrospective Recast Only) |
|--------|-----------|-----------------------------------------------------|----------------------------------------------|
| xAI | Feb 2, 2026 | 0 | 12 |
| X Holdings (via xAI) | Feb 2, 2026 (indirect) | 0 | 12 |

All 12 months of FY2025 results for both entities appear in SpaceX's audited financial statements solely through retrospective recast accounting — not through actual consolidation.

---

## Analytical Challenge Created by Retrospective Recast Accounting

The retrospective recast, mandated because all entities were under common control of Elon Musk (ASC 805-50), creates several significant analytical challenges:

### 1. Historical Financials Are Not an Operational Record
The FY2025 (and FY2024 and FY2023) financial statements present combined figures for entities that were, in reality, independently operated separate companies during those periods. An investor analyzing "SpaceX FY2025 revenue" or "SpaceX FY2025 operating loss" is looking at a retrospective accounting construct, not a record of what SpaceX shareholders actually experienced. Only from February 2, 2026 onward do the consolidated results reflect actual combined operations.

### 2. Intercompany Eliminations Obscure True Economics
The filing states that "all historical related party transactions between the entities have been eliminated in consolidation," but no quantitative disclosure of the magnitude of these eliminations is provided. If, for example, SpaceX provided launch services to xAI at preferential rates, or xAI paid X for data access, those flows vanish from the recast financials — yet they were real economic relationships between entities operating independently. The investor has no way to assess what the standalone financials looked like versus the combined view.

### 3. No Standalone vs. Combined Comparison Available
The filing does not present what SpaceX's financials would look like on a standalone basis (excluding xAI and X) alongside the recast combined figures. Without this decomposition, an investor cannot determine the relative contribution of the legacy launch/satellite business versus the newer AI and social-media segments to historical revenue, costs, or profitability.

### 4. Hybrid Equity Treatment Creates Confusion
A particularly subtle challenge: while SpaceX common stock is recast as if it always existed in the combined form, xAI's Redeemable Convertible Preferred Stock was *not* retrospectively recast for periods prior to the xAI Merger. It is presented as outstanding preferred stock in historical periods and only converted as of February 2, 2026. This creates a hybrid equity presentation — partly recast, partly historical — that can distort analyses of historical dilution, share counts, and earnings per share.

### 5. Investor Comparability Is Impaired
Because the recast combines entities with different cost structures, capital allocation strategies, and risk profiles under a single historical set of financials, period-over-period comparability is weakened. Revenue growth rates, margin trends, and capital expenditure patterns in FY2025 reflect the combined recast entity, not the trajectory of SpaceX's core business that investors have tracked for years.

---

## Bottom Line

For FY2025, neither xAI nor X was included in SpaceX's consolidated financial statements on an actual-close basis — both entities' results appear entirely through retrospective recast. This means that 100% of the FY2025 financials presented in the S-1 for these entities are an accounting convention rather than a record of actual consolidated operations. The first month of actual combined results is February 2026, and even then, the Q1 2026 interim period includes ~1 month of recast (January 1 – February 1, 2026) alongside only ~2 months of actual consolidated operations.
